unit_name: Transforms and Sums of Random Variables
hook: You already know how to describe a single random variable using its PDF or CDF — but what happens when you add two dice rolls, take the maximum of several measurements, or apply a function to a random quantity? Individual distributions only tell part of the story. This unit gives you the tools to track randomness through operations, unlocking the ability to analyze the behavior of sums, extremes, and transformations that appear everywhere from statistics to engineering.
concept: The unifying idea is that probability distributions transform predictably when we apply operations to random variables — and mastering those transformation rules lets us handle complex, real-world random systems. Formally, this unit develops the theory of distribution-preserving mappings and convolution, centered on the key idea that structure is maintained under well-defined operations on random variables.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Moment Generating Functions for Continuous Distributions
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to compute and use the moment generating function $M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]$ to compactly encode all moments of a continuous distribution and simplify the analysis of sums of random variables.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Convolutions and Sums of Independent Random Variables
topic_roadmap.description: Master the convolution technique to find the exact distribution of $X + Y$ when $X$ and $Y$ are independent, connecting integral methods with the MGF shortcut from the previous topic.
Apply the change-of-variables formula and Jacobian method to derive the distribution of $g(X)$ or $g(X, Y)$, enabling you to handle any smooth function applied to one or more random variables.

You will learn to find the distribution of the $k$-th smallest value in a random sample — including the minimum and maximum — building directly on your transformation and CDF skills to analyze ranked outcomes.
